data.title: T-Mobile: Don't Lose Faith Now
data.description:
Despite signs of expense control pressures and increased CapEx guidance, I reiterated my buy rating on T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS) back in late October. The company had strong customer metrics and solid top-line growth, but as you can see in the below chart, the stock saw notable weakness until just recently. Nevertheless, as of this writing, T-Mobile has shown some net gains since the publication of my previous update. With the company having reported their latest results just around a week ago, today I'll be conducting a fresh analysis on their fundamentals.

Below, it is shown that Q4 was a decent but mixed quarter. Strong customer metrics and top-line numbers were a bit offset by weaker cost and expense control, but business momentum looked respectable overall. Moving forward, 2026 guidance is also respectable, and the company actually offered additional outlook metrics in their Capital Markets Day that the market found encouraging. With the valuation at quite historically depressed levels, I would say that the risk/reward is decently favorable, and thus I'm reiterating my buy rating on T-Mobile.

Customer Metrics Analysis

We'll start off today with a close look at some of their key user metrics as they provide important clues regarding the company's business health. For postpaid accounts, there has been an 11% increase YoY, but do note that acquisition effects contributed to this growth. T-Mobile stated that net additions were near unchanged from the year-ago quarter, so organic growth may be a little sluggish. Nonetheless, I would say that the company has posted solid postpaid account numbers, as robust gross account addition helped to offset account deactivation.

In terms of postpaid ARPA and postpaid phone ARPU, there were quite promising results in my view. The former saw a YoY increase of 2.7% while the latter saw a 2.0% jump. I would say that this is impressive, especially with consumer confidence in the U.S. still being rather weak. These metrics indicate customers are willing to spend more. Early signs of business weakness could include people choosing cheaper, lower-margin services, but as of now there really aren't any clear signs this is happening.

One potentially concerning metric would be their postpaid phone churn. As you can see on the right, this metric edged up considerably YoY from 0.92% to 1.02% as a signal that customers are switching to competitors. However, at this point, there's no need to panic in my view. The company attributed this unfavorable development to "higher industry switching" and there are signs that this is indeed happening. In my recent article on AT&T (T), I also found that churn increased notably YoY. Therefore, it seems that customers are shopping around to find deals. This may be a signal of weak consumer confidence, although the ARPU strength should be reassuring to investors.

The last customer metric I want to mention is their broadband customer count. YoY, there has been a massive 47% increase. As shown on the right, net additions were up notably YoY as a sign of increasing momentum, even if this is partly driven by Metronet and Lumos acquisition effects. In this unit, they also saw lower 5G broadband churn, and so it seems that T-Mobile is offering good value to customers. In the long run, a strong value proposition is crucial for success, and there are good indications this is happening in their broadband unit.

Revenue Growth Looking Good

Finally, we arrive at T-Mobile's financial performance. Starting off with the top line, the company saw robust results. In Q4, they generated a total of $24.33 billion in revenues, and that represents a growth rate of 11.26% YoY. As you can see, this is a significant acceleration from the increase in the previous quarter, so there are indeed signs that business activity is overall seeing strong momentum. Furthermore, they also beat consensus expectations by $65.93 million as another sign that there was outperformance in the quarter. For their service revenues in particular, there was a very respectable 10% increase, so their core business has remained solid after a 9% increase for this metric was reported in their Q3.

Cost & Expense Control

Overall, cost control for T-Mobile probably could have been better. As a percentage of service revenues, the cost of services is up from 15.5% to 16.2% YoY even when excluding special items like their merger-related costs. In my previous article, I saw this metric drop from 15.9% to 15.4% as a sign of good cost control, so Q4 may have been a step in the wrong direction. Moving forward, investors should look for the company to restore profitability, as bottom-line figures are shown to be a bit ugly a little later in this article.

For expense control, there was a similar trend to cost control. SG&A expense as a percentage of service revenues increased from 31.9% to 32.6% YoY even when excluding special items. Recall from the beginning of the article that in my previous update, I also highlighted shaky expense control. Judging from their Q4 results, it seems that this has continued, so this will be another drag on their profitability and bottom-line performance.

Speaking of the bottom line, for Q4 T-Mobile reported an adjusted EPS of $0.41. While this represented a 6.7% drop, they were still able to beat analyst expectations by a good margin of $0.41, so things could have obviously been much worse. Moving forward, things may get worse for the bottom before things get better, as consensus calls for a rather steep drop in their 2026 Q1. In fact, we're turning to their outlook next.

Full-Year Outlook

T-Mobile provided guidance for 2026 as a whole. I will go over a few metrics here. For postpaid net account additions, they expect 950K at the midpoint. This comes after 2025's 1.2 million in net additions, so there may be a slight decrease in momentum in their business. Do keep in mind that they had a significant number of acquisitions in 2025, so this comparison may not be a great one.

For core adjusted EBITDA, the midpoint of the range shown on the right implies a 10% increase YoY. This would outpace the 7% increase for 2025, so core profitability is set to improve. Given the cost and expense control discussion above, this is a good sign for investors.

Lastly, for CapEx, there is expected to be little change from 2025, and as you can see, the company's cash flows show that this level of capital spending shouldn't be an issue. As a whole, I find 2026 guidance to be respectable and indicative of a resilient fiscal year.

Valuation Offers Some Opportunity

Despite the recent rebound, the forward P/E ratio remains below the 20.59 reading in the previous update. At a current level of 18.88, I believe there is some opportunity for investors.

Today, it was shown that business momentum has been quite strong for T-Mobile as both customer metrics and top-line figures were solid. However, as a result of shaky cost and expense management, the bottom line suffered. After some mixed Q4 results, the outlook for 2026 looks overall respectable. On their Capital Markets Day, the company actually provided an additional longer-term forecast of where their business is headed, and that reportedly helped the stock to pare losses. While this additional guidance wasn't thoroughly discussed in this article, the market's reaction is a good indication that the prospects are brightening for the company.

As a whole, it seems that the business has been resilient and is well-positioned for the future. Once they get those costs and expenses back under strict control, the bottom line should see better results. At a relatively depressed reading below 20, I believe the forward P/E shows that the risk/reward at this point is decently attractive for long-term investors.

Takeaway

T-Mobile's fundamentals definitely aren't perfect currently in my view, but the valuation is low enough to make the stock worthy of consideration by investors. The company has challenges like a higher industry switching rate and business efficiency concerns, but I believe the latter is a solvable issue as their core business activity continues to head in the right direction. T-Mobile has been one of the leaders in telecom in the U.S., and at this point there are no indications that their positioning is at risk. As a result, I'm reiterating my buy rating on T-Mobile stock.

data.title: eBay Pops Following Depop Acquisition And Strong Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the past year, eBay Inc. (EBAY) has been a bit of a roller coaster, notching huge gains but also suffering sharp declines. I covered eBay back in July 2025, and shortly thereafter, the stock climbed more than 30%. A sharp selloff in late October following a tepid earnings release erased those gains, but a positive earnings release on February 18th had eBay up more than 8% in pre-market trading on the 19th as I write this. I believe there is quite a lot to like with eBay and its earnings. Thus, I rate it a Buy.

There’s a lot to like with the numbers, but perhaps the biggest and brightest move was the announced $1.2 billion acquisition of Depop from Etsy (ETSY). I covered Etsy as well in 2025, and while I came away with a Hold rating, I highlighted the potential for Depop specifically, which focuses on second-hand clothing and vintage items. Etsy fell about 20% after my Hold rating, although it gained over 15% in pre-market trading, following the deal’s announcement. Out of the gate this morning, growth for eBay was more temperate at about 3%, while ETSY opened up more than 10% over yesterday’s closing price.

The Depop Move Is Especially Attractive

The cash for Depop deal could turn out well for both parties, but I especially like the move for eBay. Not only is Depop a natural fit for eBay, but its strong positioning among younger shoppers can help eBay make inroads with a crucial demographic. As far as I can tell, eBay has a bit of a stodgy reputation, and more than 60% of its users are 35 or older according to Similarweb, which also reports that more than half of Depop’s user base is under the age of 35. Other sources point to Gen Z making up 90% of Depop’s user base, and in a report put together along with Bain & Co., the company itself notes its users are “overwhelmingly from Gen Z,” although the data is from a study conducted in 2020. More recent research from the Harris Poll, however, shows Depop’s brand equity posting strong gains among Gen Z.

Depop tends to focus on presentable listings and emphasizes fashionability. It’s sometimes described as being as much of a lifestyle app as it is a used clothing app, although that’s overstating things, in my mind. The app does have a pretty “hip” reputation, as we older folks say. This is especially important for eBay, which is already a rather popular online clothing retailer with a strong position in the resale market. I like eBay, but it’s certainly not the first thing that comes to my mind when I think “fashion.”

Crucially, younger shoppers seem more willing to buy second-hand. As an older millennial, growing up second-hand often had a bad stigma. Yet an unfriendly labor market for Gen Z, high housing costs, an increased focus on sustainability, and growing up in an era of high inflation all seem to have shifted views among younger folks. The Harris Poll mentioned earlier found that 63% of Gen Z. shoppers have bought used clothing (and 63% also mentioned price as a primary motivating factor). Another poll found that more than half of Gen Z shoppers actually prefer to buy used when possible (compared to 44% of Millennials).

The American second-hand clothing market was worth $28 billion in 2019, but has since surged to $56 billion this year, according to Thredup (a competitor). Globally, the market is expected to jump from $227 billion in 2024 to $370 billion in 2027. Clothing is only a small part of eBay’s offerings even with Depop included. However, I believe Gen Z. will also generate relatively high demand (compared to past generations) when it comes to other goods. Depop could be an excellent hook to snag younger customers who then can be looped into eBay’s wider ecosystem and product offering.

eBay Earnings and Fundamentals

I believe eBay offers one of the better values in this market. For the full year, the company beat gross merchandise value (GMV) and revenue expectations, as illustrated above. Profit matched the high expectations, as did the diluted non-GAAP EPS. Most of the wins weren’t mind-blowing, but better to edge out expectations than failing to meet them. GMV growth has shown some acceleration since the first half of the year.

As for Depop, the company continues to sizzle in many areas, although profitability remains a challenge. The company saw Gross Merchandise Sold grow nearly 38% YOY in Q4 2025 and added 3.8 million users. However, it posted an annual loss in excess of $50 million. This number doesn’t really worry me, but hopefully eBay can sort out profits sooner rather than later.

The ~19 ratio is quite appetizing, especially with so many companies above 30. That said, many such companies enjoy a higher profit margin, but eBay’s~ 20.37 as net income margin last reported by Seeking Alpha is nothing to sneeze at. The last reported PS ratio weighed in at 3.57, but revenue growth was rather slow at 4.36% YOY.

I’m more upbeat than my fellow Seeking Alpha analysts as a whole and also the Seeking Alpha Quant tools, which both rate Hold. The D- valuation grade provided by the quant tools strikes me as rather harsh, especially in the current environment’s high general P/E ratios. Revenue growth has been somewhat slow, so I can understand the C grade. That said, I do think growth could accelerate as consumers remain under financial pressure and turn to second-hand goods.

Why I Could Be Wrong

I suspect many consumers turn to eBay in order to protect their wallets. However, if consumers come under enough pressure, it could impact eBay. At some point, there’s simply not much juice to squeeze. With the Depop acquisition, this app could cannibalize some of eBay’s sales, weighing the company down overall. Further, Depop faces pretty stiff competition in the competitive second-hand market.

Key Takeaway: Good Fundamentals and a Smart Acquisition

I doubt consumer and especially Gen. Z woes are going to ease anytime soon, which should spur a focus on value. Depop will offer a way to onboard new customers for eBay as a whole, and it should also help eBay build its brand among younger audiences. The reasonable valuation metrics and solid profitability together cement eBay as a Buy.

data.title: Sandisk Is Grossly Overvalued As Memory Chip Shortage Likely Transitory
data.description:
I still remember when Nvidia (NVDA) was “it” about a year ago after going up 1000% in about a year. Now the market’s attention has turned to Sandisk (SNDK) as the stock rose from a low of $27/share in April 2025 to a high of $725/share a few weeks ago. I believe SNDK is probably unsuitable for retail investors at these valuations and would caution retail investors to avoid buying. This article explains why.

SNDK’s fundamentals do not justify such a run-up on the stock price

SNDK generated annual revenues of $6-7bn a year from FY23 to FY25 (with FY22 a bit higher at $9.7bn). SNDK was delisted in 2016, and before then, generated revenues of around $5-6bn a year in FY13-14. So SNDK has not really grown its revenues in over a decade.

SNDK’s market share in 2025 is about 12%, very close to where it was in 2013. So in terms of market share, SNDK is a major player holding its ground, but far from being as dominant as NVDA or ASML or TSMC. Currently, SNDK has a market cap of $92 bn and the “consensus” 2026 net profit estimate is around $6bn (calculated using the market cap divided by the 2026 consensus P/E estimate of 15), mainly based on AI’s increased demand for memory chips in the data storage market.

1. Shortages do not maketh a business

There’s been a lot of talk of memory chip shortages in the past year. While shortages can earn corporations (and fortunate individuals) a windfall in the short run, shortages do not make a strong business model in the long run.

For example, Coca Cola (KO) does not have a $342bn market cap because there is a shortage of Coca Cola. On the contrary, supply of Coca Cola (and any drinks under its umbrella) can be viewed as unlimited. KO earns a healthy profit margin because of consumer stickiness (i.e. people who are used to KO don’t buy Pepsi or other drinks as often). Similarly in the tech business, Google search engine does not “limit” the number of searches that a user can perform; it earns profits because users cannot be bothered to try out alternatives such as Bing even if Microsoft (MSFT) spends a huge effort on pushing Bing. Consumer stickiness is the underlying bedrock behind these and many other successful business models.

Shortages are often rebalanced much faster than expected (that’s what markets are supposed to do, after all). In the heat of the moment, it feels like the shortage of silver or tin or oil or auto chips or toilet paper (during the COVID pandemic) or what-have-you has no end in sight, and suddenly people find ingenious ways to reduce the shortage either by bringing new supply online or reducing usage.

In fact, during the Everything Bubble of 2021, there was a lot of talk about a shortage of auto chips as semiconductor foundries shifted production to consumer digital products. For example, I remember Texas Instruments was discussed as a major beneficiary of the chip shortage in 2021/22.

As can be seen below, TXN’s operating income did indeed surge from circa $6bn in FY19 and FY20 to $9 bn in FY21 and $10.5bn in FY22. Yet this shortage quickly evaporated, and operating income was back to $5.3bn in FY24 – the lowest since FY16! The so-called shortage during the COVID pandemic lasted for approximately 2 years and was not enduring at all.

2. Memory chip market is extremely cyclical

Another point to consider is that the memory chip market is very cyclical, as shown below (source). Even in recent years, memory chip market was down in 2019, up to 2021, down in 2023, and so on. Unlike AI chips where NVDA has a dominant position in the market that is not loosening in the foreseeable future (even though I am bearish over the long term, but that’s another story), SNDK is nowhere near such a dominant position in the memory chip market and will not be insulated from the downcycle once it hits. In fact, the most recent “story” that argues memory chips are in a shortage because of AI suspiciously sounds like a repeat of the “story” in 2022 that memory chips were in a shortage because of foundries directing capacity to consumer digital products.

Major shareholders are selling

Even more telling, major shareholders are selling! SNDK’s former parent, Western Digital (WDC) is offering 5.8 million shares in a secondary offering at $545/share. After the sale, WDC will only retain 1.7 million SNDK shares, which it plans to dispose of later. So WDC is selling most of the SNDK shares it owns and publicly communicates its intention to sell the remainder.

None of these sales proceeds will go to SNDK to fund R&D or capex; it’s completely a cash-out by the existing shareholder (that originally spun-off and listed SNDK at around $40-50/share in 2025).

I believe this is a major tell, it’s as if your neighbor on the poker table is showing you he has a winning hand before going all-in, yet you’re hoping to catch a winning card on the river.

Valuation

Providing a valuation estimate for SNDK is tricky given the significant uncertainty in how long the current memory chip boom can go. According to Seeking Alpha, the current consensus estimate is that the P/E drops to 7.4 by 2027. It appears to me SNDK's current market cap is essentially embedding expectations that SNDK will earn windfall profits for another 7 years, all the way until 2034, which seems to me a bit aggressive to extrapolate that far, given the AI boom has only gone on for about two years, and I think it's unlikely SNDK will be earning these windfall profits for that many years.

However, the moment supply no longer outstrips demand and business goes back to usual, SNDK's market cap might just revert to (i) $7bn market cap before the bull move plus (ii) any cash it earned during the windfall period, which suggests to me that 90% of its current market cap is being driven by expectations of windfall periods continuing many years in an extremely favorable market condition, and this proposition does not seem to me to be a good bet for individual investors.

Risks to bearish thesis

In the unlikely event that the memory chip shortage goes on for a long, long time, maybe there is a sliver of a chance SNDK’s current valuation could be justified. Obviously I don’t think that’s likely, but I have to act as my own devil’s advocate, so there’s that.

Conclusion

I would suggest individual investors approach SNDK with extreme caution and consider my devil’s advocate arguments above when contemplating whether to buy SNDK. I think SNDK’s extremely high performance in 2025 is more a sign of market exuberance rather than any strong fundamentals. When I first learned about investing, the story was that you earn maybe 8%, 9% a year if you stick with the stock market long run. Had one invested in one of the best performers in the past two decades, Alphabet (GOOG), in 2004, said investor would have earned a return of 6,160%. This is very impressive but then you realize it’s an annual compounded rate of 21.7%. In most years, GOOG just plodded along with a nice return but nowhere near the 1,000% in one year that attracts crowds in NVDA and SNDK. The action that SNDK attracts may reflect part of the investor community desperately grasping for anything that has a semblance of a story that can offer massive returns immediately, and per my experience, more often than not, such a fixation on stocks with massive, quick gains does not work out.

data.title: Capital Has Migrated From Software Companies To This Asset Class
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on assets that track the main American indices. This article is part of a weekly series where I bring valuable insights about macroeconomics and investments to Seeking Alpha readers. In this week's edition, I will analyze the fall in shares of companies with businesses threatened by artificial intelligence and contextualize this with my market vision.

Context

Firstly, it is important to remember that I described this phenomenon that investors are seeing in an article last year, where I detailed my outlook for 2026. The article was called “2026 S&P 500 Outlook: The Start Of Natural Selection In AI,” and in it, I shared my opinion that 2026 would mark the natural selection of companies in the context of artificial intelligence.

This view was so solid that I even adjusted my recommended stock portfolio. In 2025, the portfolio only included American companies, several of which were big techs, and in 2026, I exchanged some American shares for Brazilian shares. The choice of Brazil was strategic: currency appreciation, expected rise in commodities, and extremely attractive valuation, but I explain in detail in another article.

The fact is that the recommended stock portfolio easily beats the S&P 500 as emerging markets receive high investment flows. Year to date, the S&P 500 rises 1% while my recommended stock portfolio rises 5.3%.

But returning to the central point of this article, it is curious to observe how many investors are extremely concerned about the fall in the shares of companies whose businesses are threatened by artificial intelligence.

Investor skepticism was marked by the fall of software companies represented, for example, by ETF IGV, but we had other events, such as the fall in finance sector shares on Tuesday, the fall in real estate shares on Wednesday, and the fall in logistics shares on Thursday, all with skeptical investors due to the disruption of AI.

Therefore, my intention is to clarify this horizon; after all, whenever investors feel fear, opportunities appear.

Pay attention to what really matters

Investors did not understand that capital did not leave the stock market but merely migrated between asset classes. While the software companies represented by ETF IGV fell sharply during the month, the Dow Jones, on the other hand, rose; that is, capital migrated from companies whose businesses were threatened by AI to solid businesses with competitive advantages not affected by AI.

In my opinion, worried investors forget that there are several very positive signs for investments. One of them was the January jobs report, which exceeded expectations, meaning the American job market is resilient.

In parallel, inflation data from Truflation indicates that inflation in the United States has been falling sharply in recent weeks, which is another catalyst for the rise in the stock market.

Finally, a graphic really caught my attention when I decided to create this article:

The dollar is going through one of the biggest devaluations in its history and is testing 15-year support. However, according to Secretary Scott Bessent, the United States' plan is for the dollar to become stronger.

The objective is to force companies to establish themselves in the United States and not have to pay huge tariffs for producing abroad, and thus reindustrialize the American economy.

In theory, over time, government revenue from tariffs should decrease, while tax revenue will increase, as companies will produce more and more there. Thus, the strong economy and improved public accounts should bring a lot of investment to the US.

This should increase the demand for dollars; therefore, the dollar should rise. So, according to Bessent himself, don't be fooled by the fall of the dollar, as this is just a series of economies emerging from stagnation.

If the Trump administration manages to implement these fundamentals in the United States, the dollar will become increasingly stronger against other currencies, and given the economic situation that I study weekly, it seems to me that this implementation will be successful.

So, would you prefer to buy American stocks with the dollar at its current level or when it rises? Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. My target for the S&P 500 at the end of 2026 is 7778 points, and after this explanation, I will show what I really think investors should worry about and analyze in depth.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

The S&P 500's margin debt is rising, and historically, this has been a reason for strong market corrections. Will it be different this time due to the unique context of investments in artificial intelligence?

Microsoft, which is one of the big techs that has made large investments in artificial intelligence, for example, has erased all gains compared to the S&P 500 since the launch of ChatGPT in 2022.

In a parallel to this context, tax auditors from the main accounting audits must be becoming concerned as active and pending leases have risen considerably in Big Tech's balance sheets.

The Bottom Line

The last few days have been risk-averse, with skeptical investors selling shares in software companies or any other sectors with businesses possibly impacted by artificial intelligence.

In this article, I reiterated my view that a natural selection of businesses will occur in 2026, but I listed the opportunities with capital migration between asset classes. Stock picking has never been more important than in recent years.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. In my view, the risk-return ratio is still attractive; however, investors need to carefully analyze the asset classes to which their portfolio is exposed.

data.title: Insulet Q4 Earnings Review: Strong Performance Meets Sky-High Expectations
data.description:
Investment Overview

It has been some time since I last posted about Insulet Corporation (PODD) for Seeking Alpha. My last coverage was in November 2024, and I assigned the stock a Hold rating. That seems to have been the right call, as 15 months on, the stock is down just ~8%, priced at $247. Market cap is presently about $18B.

Before we consider the headline figures from Insulet's Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings, released yesterday, and forward guidance for 2026, let's remind ourselves of the company's core business activities - as per Insulet's 2025 10-K filing:

The Omnipod platform offers continuous insulin delivery that provides all the benefits of insulin pump therapy in a unique way without the need for external tubing required with conventional pumps.

The small, lightweight, self-adhesive disposable tubeless Omnipod device (“Pod”), can be worn in multiple locations, including the abdomen, hip, back of upper arm, upper thigh, or lower back.

We have designed Omnipod products to fit within the normal daily routines of users. The Pod can be worn for up to three days at a time and, because it is waterproof (with an IP28 rating for up to 25 feet for 60 minutes), there is no need to remove it when showering, swimming, or performing other activities.

Omnipod products provide for virtually pain-free automated cannula (a small flexible tube) insertion through which insulin is delivered, eliminating the need for MDI or the use of pump and tubing.

The target market is patients with Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes who are insulin-dependent, and the goal is to allow them to "live their lives and manage their diabetes with unprecedented freedom, comfort, convenience, and ease."

Below is an overview of results for the full year 2025, taken from Insulet's earnings presentation:

As we can see, revenues were up by 30% year-on-year, to $2.7bn, with operating income increasing by 54%, to $476m, and margin increasing to 17.6%, up from 14.9% in 2024. Omnipod earned the majority - $1.92bn - of its revenues in the U.S., earning $745m internationally - up 39% year-on-year.

Free cash flow was $377.7m, up 24% year-on-year. Net income was reported as $247.1m on a GAAP basis, and earnings per share were $3.51, also on a GAAP basis, down from $5.97 in 2024. Cash and equivalents of $716.1m were reported as of the end of Q4, with total current assets of $1.9bn. Total current liabilities amounted to $(680.1m), and long-term debt, $930.8m.

Guidance for 2026 is shared in the below slide:

Analysis - Strong Performance, Strong Guidance Impresses

On its earnings call yesterday, Insulet CEO Ashley McEvoy noted that the company had delivered "our 10th consecutive year of 20% or greater constant currency revenue growth." This is an impressive feat that deserves credit, in my view, and it seems as though the run will continue in 2026, so long as guidance is met.

The CEO also advised that:

We achieved record new customer starts across both the U.S. and international in the fourth quarter and for the full year, with the vast majority coming from people transitioning from multiple daily injections.

This reflects growing provider confidence, which, as I just mentioned, is driven by strong clinical evidence and consistent real-world outcomes. Importantly, it also reinforces that Insulet is not only the market leader in AID, but also the clear driver of overall market expansion, and we intend to maintain this leadership position.

Emphasizing the size of its largest market, US T1D, the CEO added:

The U.S. type 1 market is a more than $9 billion opportunity with AID penetration at just 40%, which is well behind CGM penetration of 70%.

Our U.S. prescriber base now includes more than 30,000 health care professionals, up approximately 28% year-over-year. The strength and reach of our commercial teams position this segment to remain a meaningful and consistent contributor to our global customer growth.

In my November 2024 analysis, I had noted that Omnipod 5 had secured approval for both T1D and T2D patients, the T2D approval adding ~6m people to the addressable market, 2.5m of whom are insulin-intensive and on multiple daily injections. Yesterday, the CEO noted that:

Our type 2 prescriber base grew 62% in 2025 to now more than 6,500 clinicians... in a type 2 market of more than $12 billion, where AID (automated insulin delivery) penetration remains below 5%, which is far behind roughly 55% of CGM adoption, stronger education, improved outcomes and increasing access are already accelerating adoption.

Our U.S. sales force, which is the largest in the industry, reaches high-prescribing offices that treat both type 1 and type 2 diabetes, giving us confidence in our ability to unlock the next 5% to 10% of type 2 penetration efficiently.

In summary, there are seemingly plenty of tailwinds helping to drive Insulet's business forward, and we can list some more highlights discussed during yesterday's call.

Product availability in 48k U.S. pharmacies, covered for >90% of insured lives, the fact that most users pay "about $1 a day through our pay-as-you-go model and preferred formulary position," strong growth in Europe, with revenues up 39% on a constant exchange rate ("CER") basis, and recent Omnipod launches in Canada, Australia, and the Middle East, and an upcoming launch in Spain.

Looking further ahead, there is a fully-closed loop offering planned for 2028, and work on Omnipod 6 is ongoing, with a pivotal clinical study, STRIVE, now completed, and a third manufacturing plant in Costa Rica under development, to accompany existing plants in Acton, Massachusetts, and Johor, Malaysia.

Analysis - Is Future Success Already Priced Into Insulet's Valuation?

As impressive as Insulet's Q4 and full-year 2025 results were, we could make the case that the company's valuation is on the high side. A Market cap of $17.4bn at the time of writing translated to a price-to-sales ("P/S") ratio of ~6.5x, while using the basic EPS figure quoted by Seeking Alpha of $3.51, we get a price-to-earnings ("P/E") ratio of ~71x.

I made a similar point in my last analysis of Insulet, noting that:

We have seen with DexCom (DXCM) that in the integrated CGM ("iCGM") industry, the market can punish companies when growth starts to plateau, which is unfair in some ways - plateauing growth is almost inevitable once a company reaches a certain size, and valuation growth

Yesterday, Insulet's Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") commented re the 2026 guidance:

I know you will all ask me why is growth decelerating? Just a couple of quick notes on this. First, this year, we will be anniversarying the first full year of the U.S. launch of Omnipod for type 2, which was a significant contributor to last year's performance. In addition, we're beginning to annualize several of our international launches, which continue to ramp well, but create more challenging year-over-year comparisons. These year-over-year comp dynamics are reflected in our 2026 guidance.

An analyst also posed the question, "How are you feeling about being able to sustain the 20% growth in light of new competition?"

In its 10-K filing, Insulet notes:

We compete with established companies that produce insulin pumps, such as Medtronic Diabetes, a division of Medtronic plc (MDT) (which division is being spun out into a new, independent publicly traded company), Tandem Diabetes Care Inc. (TNDM), as well as emerging companies like Beta Bionics Inc (BBNX) Our competitors may develop products in the future that are superior to ours which would inhibit our ability to compete effectively.

I suspect the analyst was referring to Beta Bionics, which completed its IPO in January last year. CEO McEvoy pointed to the $3bn of investment Insulet has made to date, high barriers to market entry, Insulet's weight of study data, and relationships with payers and pharmacy benefit managers ("PBMs") and concluded by saying, "Omnipod has been at this for 9 years, and we'll continue to have unparalleled access and affordability."

Another analyst queried the slightly disappointing recent share price performance - stock is down 25% on a six-month basis (at the time of writing), and while it is up an impressive >800% on a ten-year basis, on a five-year basis, it is down ~9.5%.

As such, to answer my own question posed above, I do think that Insulet's valuation today suggests the market anticipates many more years of >20% revenue - and earnings - growth, and I do believe these expectations may be difficult to meet for the company.

While I agree with management's comments that markets are far from fully penetrated and that Omnipod is currently "#1 prescribed and #1 most requested," competitive threats are significant - Medtronic is a medtech giant with deep resources, while the emergence of a new challenger in Beta Bionics is an unwanted development for Insulet, I'd expect, that could lead to, e.g., pricing wars and increased competition to be favored by PBMs.

Concluding Thoughts: I'm Maintaining My Hold Rating On Insulet Based On Market's High Expectations

My last Hold call on Insulet stock proved to be the right one, and I am upholding it for mostly the same reasons I assigned it in the first place.

While there may be good reasons why Insulet's revenues guidance is only narrowly above the 20% mark for 2026, the challenge of continuing to meet such growth expectations as the revenue numbers get bigger and bigger is not one I am certain Insulet can meet.

Yes, markets are underpenetrated, but we may also note that patients do have alternative options, and not just other suppliers of AID devices. The GLP-1 drug class diabetes/weight loss revolution is in full swing, and although there is no reason why a patient could not be on a GLP-1 drug and using Omnipod, it's possible GLP-1 use could shrink the T2D opportunity for AIDs, in my view.

All things considered, when we look at recent share price performance, we could make the case that the market's appetite for Insulet's growth story is waning, and the fact the share price was largely unmoved by the promise of another year of >20% revenues growth suggests to me that a downward correction may occur if that guidance is not met for any reasons - supply chain issues, new competitors, slower than expected international growth, saturation in the U.S. market, under-penetration in T2D etc.

While it's also true that a guidance hike mid-year could trigger a bull run on stock, as revenue targets grow higher and higher, I view this as harder and harder to achieve for Insulet, and therefore I remain on the sidelines in 2026, as I was in 2025.

Highlights

  • During the fourth quarter of 2025, the largest portfolio sector weightings were Information Technology and Industrials. The largest sector overweight was Health Care and the largest sector underweight was Information Technology.
  • The Health Care sector contributed the most to relative performance while the Communication Services sector that detracted the most from relative performance.

Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross-currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index-level surface. The market’s biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure. Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Portfolio Update

Class A shares of the Alger Dynamic Opportunities Fund (SPEDX) underperformed the S&P 500 Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. For the quarter, the portfolio’s average long exposure was 81.57% and the average short exposure was -32.10%. The portfolio’s average cash exposure was 50.53%. Among long positions, the Information Technology and Health Care sectors were the largest absolute sector weightings, while the Health Care and Communication Services sectors were the largest relative sector weightings. During the quarter, long positions, in aggregate, detracted from both absolute and relative performance. Short positions, in aggregate, detracted from both absolute and relative performance.

Long positions Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Natera, Inc. (NTRA), and MongoDB, Inc. (MDB) were among the top contributors to performance.

  • Alphabet is the parent of Google and a global leader in digital advertising, cloud computing, and AI-driven consumer services. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as investors increasingly recognized improving execution across the company’s AI strategy, supported by strong engagement trends and rising AI-related workloads. Google Cloud was a key driver, with management highlighting accelerating usage, including increased AI token processing and continued momentum in the Gemini ecosystem. Sentiment was further supported by strengthening cloud demand and new customer wins, as well as reports that Google may broaden access to its TPU capabilities, including potential deployments beyond Google Cloud.
  • Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.
  • MongoDB is a software company that provides a document-oriented database platform, enabling developers to store and manage data in flexible structures compared with traditional SQL databases. The platform is designed for agility and scalability, supporting rapid application development and a wide range of data types and workloads. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, including faster revenue growth, rising customer adoption for AI-related workloads, and continued progress on profitability. Management highlighted that Atlas, the company’s flagship cloud database service, is growing roughly 30% year over year.

Our short position in a rare-disease biopharmaceutical company contributed positively to performance. The company develops and commercializes therapies for rare metabolic and neurobehavioral disorders. During the quarter, shares declined after management acknowledged that launch momentum had slowed, reflected in fewer new patient starts and higher discontinuations. The selloff intensified as investors reset expectations for the pace and durability of uptake, with additional pressure from follow-on legal and investigative headlines tied to launch metrics.

Long positions Meta Platforms Inc. (META), Nebius Group (NBIS), and Roblox Corp. (RBLX) were among the top detractors from performance.

  • Meta is the world’s largest social-media company, operating platforms that include Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Shares detracted during the quarter as investors focused on management’s guidance for materially higher operating expenses and capital expenditures (CapEx) tied to AI infrastructure investments. Management noted that 2026 CapEx is expected to be “notably” higher than 2025, which pressured forward earnings and free-cash-flow expectations despite strong underlying results. While the company highlighted continued advertising momentum and indicated that revenue growth could remain robust into year-end, investors remained concerned about the magnitude and timing of the AI-related CapEx.
  • Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.
  • Roblox is a digital platform where users play, learn, communicate, and expand relationships in millions of 3D digital experiences within an open-world metaverse environment. The platform is powered by user-generated content (UGC) with experiences and most items being built by third-party developers and creators, on top of Roblox’s development engine. Roblox uses AI to improve recommendations, moderate user-generated content, and help creators build new experiences more efficiently. The company reports bookings when users purchase its virtual currency, Robux, through one-time purchases, prepaid cards, or subscriptions. Shares detracted during the quarter following management’s decision to use the surge in engagement and monetization to reinvest into the business through higher capital expenditures and operating expenses. The company plans to allocate capital into building and training their own AI models along with incorporating additional safeguards to weed out inappropriate activities.

Our short position in a U.S. electric-vehicle manufacturer detracted from performance. During the quarter, the company posted better-than-expected results as manufacturing execution improved and cost actions supported progress toward stronger vehicle margins, while a strategic partnership with a large European automaker reinforced confidence in its technology roadmap and funding outlook. Shares appreciated on improved sentiment around the path to profitability and balance-sheet support, negatively impacting our short position.

data.title: Pool Corporation: Still Expensive With No Rebound In Sight
data.description:
Shares of Pool Corporation (POOL) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about a quarter of their value. While maintenance activity and market share gains have helped to provide some support for results, new pool construction activity has been weak as consumers limit spending on large projects. These pressures were apparent in Q4, with the company disappointing investors with its results and outlook. I last covered POOL in November when I reiterated shares as a "Sell," and they are down 7% since then. With updated financials and increased optimism about housing activity in 2026, now is a good time to revisit POOL.

Operating expenses pressure margins

In the company’s fourth quarter, Pool Corporation earned $0.85 per share, which was $0.12 below expectations as revenue fell slightly to $982 million. For the full year, EBITDA declined by about $26 million to $654 million. For the full year, the company earned $10.85, which included $0.12 of tax benefits. This was down 3% from 2024 as construction levels remain weak and expansion efforts pressure margins. Results do not offer any sign that the business is about to rebound significantly.

Now, thanks to pricing actions, gross margins improved by 70 bps to 30.1%. As a result, gross profit increased by 2% to $296 million. Offsetting this, operating expenses rose by 6% to $244 million, a sharp pace considering the lack of revenue growth. Higher employee and health insurance costs have weighed. Additionally, the company has been opening more POOL360 locations, though with flat revenue, they are not yet contributing sufficient sales, leading to incremental margin pressure. As a result of this expense pressure, operating income fell by 14% to $52 million.

It is important to remember that only a minority of Pool’s business is tied to the construction of pools. Most of its business is for maintenance work, chemicals, etc. While there is still some cyclicality here, as consumers can defer maintenance for a time, much of this spending is nondiscretionary. On the other hand, new construction is very cyclical, dependent on both home construction and existing homeowners deciding to add a pool to their backyard.

Construction fundamentals are still negative

With affordability pressures top of mind, the willingness to undertake such a large expense has been greatly reduced. Pool construction is also typically debt-financed (with consumers often using home equity lines of credit), and interest rates remain high. As you can see below, mortgage rates have been ticking lower relative to their peaks, but they have been stubbornly holding above 6%. In general, I expect we would need to see the 10-year treasury decisively decline below 4% to lead to mortgage rates dropping below 6%. This is not my base case, and I expect home renovation work (including pool construction) to stay muted.

Beyond this, the number of homes currently under construction continues to fall, and this creates fewer opportunities for new pools. With the housing market particularly soft in Texas and Florida (two critical pool markets), construction is likely to be weaker there for longer. Given my view that home prices are likely to be flat to down slightly in 2026, I expect construction to continue to decline, at least through Q3 2026. This limits Pool’s potential growth.

Capital returns will continue

Now, it is important to note that Pool is still a very cash-generative business thanks to its reliance on maintenance activity. Free cash flow was $309 million in 2025. However, this was weighed down by about $125 million of working capital needs as it built inventory. Absent this, run-rate free cash flow was about $435 million. I expect to see working capital pressures reverse to an extent in 2026, likely flattering free cash flow.

Inventories are up 13% to $1.5 billion, and faster inventory growth than sales growth is a warning sign for me. That said, Pool is not like a retailer that will need to mark down clothes to move them ahead of the next season. Management pulled forward some commodity purchases to front-run supplier price increases, so I would expect inventories to gradually work back down. Still, this bears monitoring.

Importantly, Pool maintains a solid balance sheet, though debt increased by $250 million in 2025 to $1.2 billion. Net debt/EBITDA leverage has ticked up as a result to 1.7x. As EBITDA has declined and POOL has continued returning capital to investors, we have seen its balance sheet slowly but steadily weaken. However, this has come from an extremely strong place, and I view 1.5-2x as an entirely healthy place to operate. POOL’s leverage is still consistent with this. I see limited scope for debt-funded buybacks, but Pool can use direct free cash flow to capital returns.

During 2025, POOL repurchased $341 million of stock. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 2.7% from last year. Shares also offer a 2% dividend yield, and Pool has increased its dividend for 15 straight years. Pool’s dividend costs about $185 million, which is safely covered by free cash flow. I expect at least $250 million of buybacks in 2026, which should reduce the share count by a further ~2.5%.

Outlook points to further downside

Alongside results, the company offered guidance for 2026 and is expecting EPS to be $10.85-$11.15. Consensus was $11.65, so this guidance is quite light relative to analyst expectations. I have argued that housing-related activity is likely to disappoint many betting on a rebound this year, and this is another data point consistent with that view. This guidance does point to modest EPS growth. However, that is largely due to a lower share count, and guidance implies that net income will be essentially flat in 2026 vs. 2025.

I believe that business is stabilizing but not yet rebounding. I view guidance as being largely consistent with this. Barring a recession, we should not see pool construction materially worsen from 2025, and we likely are finding a floor around current levels. Rather, with the broader macro environment, it is difficult to see the case for a significant rebound. Back in November, I was targeting $10.75-$11.75 in 2026 EPS, which drove my $230 price target using a 20x earnings multiple. POOL’s midpoint is actually a bit below my expectations.

I now view $10.80-$11.30 as the likely range for 2026 earnings, given my view that construction activity will be stable around current levels. Shares are now about 20x 2026 earnings. I view the large decline in shares today as the market finally recognizing that a meaningful recovery is not likely until 2027 or later. I have been bearish on POOL for some time, given my view that its valuation was structurally too high, and its multiple is finally compressing to a more reasonable level. I have argued previously that 18x is a fair multiple for this business, and I see an increasing possibility that shares can get there (whereas I previously viewed 20x as a more reasonable target). That suggests a downside of about $200.

At 20x, there is a greater argument for rating shares a “hold” with the business stabilizing. However, with limited growth likely through 2027 as the housing cycle is set to disappoint, I view the risks as skewed to the downside, especially with a wave of negative analyst revisions likely. I see shares pushing towards $200, and as a result, I retain a “Sell” on POOL.

data.title: Stop Reading Dell's Margin Story Wrong, The Math Says 50% Upside
data.description:
Introduction

In the midst of the AI frenzy, Dell (DELL) was one of the hottest stocks. Now that we are seeing a capex-fear-driven rotation out of the hyperscalers and their supply chain into energy, materials, consumer staples, industrials, and utilities, Dell appears as a stock that investors are leaving behind. We can see below that most sectors in the S&P 500 are up considerably YTD, with only a handful of laggards that are not truly down. However, sectors such as technology, communication services, and financials have a larger weight on the index, as we all know. This is why, overall, the S&P 500 is barely in the green, while the equal-weighted index (RSP) is up 6.5% YTD, which was an idea I discussed when I shared my 2026 market outlook, talking about "the revenge of the S&P 493."

Where Dell Currently Sits

I started covering Dell when I chose it as one of the top 3 picks for 2024, and it was, indeed, the best-performing one among the three. In case you have no idea how the company is organized and structured, I suggest reading one of my past articles. But, just to keep things simpler, be aware that Dell operates in the hardware business and splits its operations into two segments: Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG) and Client Solutions Group (CSG). We could say that Dell is more or less positioned in the middle of the tech value chain. It sources components and then designs and assembles them to build servers (in particular, AI-optimized servers) and to provide network equipment and storage solutions. This is what falls in the ISG business. CSG, on the other hand, focuses on what makes Dell more renowned: notebooks, desktops, and peripheral devices. Both segments generate more or less 60% of their sales in the Americas, as we read in its FY25 10-K. At the end of the last fiscal year, ISG reported net revenues of $43.6B, up 29% YoY, and managed to increase its operating margin by 20 bps to 12.8% (FY24: 12.6%; FY23: 13.2%). CSG reported $48.4B, down 1% YoY, with an operating margin of 6.1%, which represents a 150 bps decline YoY. So, more or less, CSG accounted for 55% of total revenues, while ISG represented the remaining 45%.

However, when we look at Dell's last quarter (Q3 2026), we see that ISG reported quarterly revenues of $14.1B and 9M revenues of $41.2B, surpassing CSG, which stood at $12.5B for the quarter and $37.5B for the first nine months of the year. This highlights the huge change that Dell is going through, as it is now transforming into an AI-infrastructure supplier rather than just a notebook manufacturer. We also see that servers and networking are the booming category, which grew 43% YoY on a 9M basis. However, we also see that margins, as said above, are declining, with ISG reporting a Q3 12.4% operating margin (Q3 FY25: 13.3%), which, in any case, is accretive to the 9M average. In fact, Dell's operating margin QoQ improved by 360bps (it was at 8.8% at the end of Q2 FY26), which is something that Dell's management highlighted at the UBS Global Technology and AI Conference 2025, believing that the market missed this. In other words, Dell saw its gross margin go down, but thanks to its scale, it is actually growing its operating income.

CSG, on the other hand, is just business as usual, with mild growth and mid-single-digit margins. So, it is clear that Dell's story is tightly linked to the economics of its servers and networking business.

Since the time I started following the stock, I saw people say that Dell could look like a value trap, but I believe it is not. However, we have just seen that there is one issue that prevents the stock from moving north. Although Dell delivered record revenues and EPS and reported a surging backlog, its margins don't reflect the same trend. Demand is booming, ASPs are rising, but profitability is not expanding. This happens because AI-optimized servers are actually margin dilutive right now due to rising GPU costs, which Dell passes through.

Now, Dell has confirmed that its target is a 10% ISG margin, which is, more or less, where the 9M print is. In other words, Dell admits that AI servers are margin-dilutive (they are in the mid-single-digit range), but ISG is not only servers; it is also networking and storage. As a matter of fact, Dell wants investors to understand that it is not just a hardware provider but a company that is a full-stack player. Let's read this quote from the UBS Conference I mentioned above:

It's that upfront engineering that is really differentiating us, the ability to optimize for power, optimize for performance. We look at performance per watt, performance per dollar driving density, which then gets you into the components of the design around the rack scale architecture itself, into the power, the cooling, density, its serviceability, the ability to manage that. We build management software around that. And then we have the services that deploy, install, and support it, and then financing for those customers that need it.

Dell sells lifecycle value, and this leads us to what we should factor in when we run our analysis. Right now, Dell is winning market share in data-center AI-optimized servers. The company can afford revenue growth and margin dilution (by the way, it is still profitable and doesn't sell any servers at a loss) because it is securing recurring revenues down the road. Right now, it is key to hold the best spot in the infrastructure buildout. As more and more servers are from Dell, the company is set to earn service and maintenance revenue later on. In addition, it is in the best position to sell new servers when it is time for replacement.

Now, here is the catch. When we look at its last fiscal year, we see that Dell splits its net revenues between products and services. The former item reports a gross margin of 15.8%, while the latter achieves a gross margin of 41.4%. Services are almost 2.6 times more profitable per revenue dollar compared to products. This means that when Dell grows its installed base, it is basically gaining high-margin revenues for the future.

Right now, Dell's backlog is around $18.4B, and by the time the company releases its Q4 FY26 report, I expect it to be around $25B (after all, if the hyperscalers are pouring over $650B in capex this year, someone is going to get orders). But what we have to consider here is a J-curve profitability scenario, where scale and services will play a major role. Currently, the products/services mix sees products between 75% and 79%, with services contributing to the company's revenues in the range between 21% and 25% (I am using FY25 data and 9M26 data). In a "land-and-expand" process, we are in the landing phase where products gain share and weigh the revenue mix. But when we move gradually on to the expansion phase, we could see a 70/30 split, which I consider easily achievable. Now, if the numbers we have seen above hold true, for every billion in revenue that Dell is able to shift from products to services, we have around $250M in additional gross profit without even increasing sales volumes. I think this tailwind should not be underestimated. Dell is building an installed base that will generate service revenues that will probably surprise many investors.

Dell's Valuation

Dell currently guides for around $112B in revenues and EPS just shy of $10. I think that the chances of a beat are high, and this is why you will see that my DCF model starts with FY26 revenues of $113B. After all, I am expecting ISG to grow around 34% YoY and CSG to grow around 3.5% YoY. These are not bold numbers (Dell itself is guiding for ISG revenue growth of mid-to-high thirties). Its EPS guidance seems fair, but if we factor in that Dell is actually already flexing its scale, I think we could end up with EPS of $10.10. After all, Dell has a $10B buyback program and returned $1.6B in Q3 alone. This means the company is decreasing its share count, and this could help its EPS reach the psychological threshold of $10.00. If this is the case, the company is trading at a TTM PE of 11.7, which is not that expensive.

When we run a DCF model, we see that I am assuming the company will have an FCF margin between 4.7% and 5.6% through 2035. Using a WACC of 8.8% and starting from an unlevered FCF of $5.3B for FY26, we end up calculating that the present value of the cash flows through 2035 is $57.7B. Then, I use a 2.5% perpetual growth rate and calculate a terminal value of $87.6B. This leads to an EV of $145.3B; we then subtract debt and add cash, and we have an equity value of $124.3B. Divided by the 698M shares outstanding, we have an intrinsic value per share of $178, which I believe positions Dell as a compelling opportunity offering up to a 52% upside. Even when we use a higher WACC of 9.6%, we have an intrinsic value of $153, which is still a 31% upside from today's price. Since I believe the earnings call could report a strong beat, I think buying Dell before the earnings report next week (February 26) is a wise move, and this is why I will be initiating a position.

data.title: The State Of REITs: February 2026 Edition
data.description:
REIT Performance

REITs kicked off 2025 with a solid +1.09% average total return in January. The REIT sector’s January performance was largely in line with the broader market. REITs outperformed the NASDAQ (+1.0%), but fell short of the S&P 500 (+1.4%) and Dow Jones Industrial Average (+1.8%). The market cap-weighted Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) outpaced the average REIT in January (+2.61% vs. +1.09%). The spread between the 2026 FFO multiples of large-cap REITs (16.4x) and small-cap REITs (12.8x) widened in January as multiples expanded 0.5 turns for large caps and only 0.1 turns for small caps. Investors currently need to pay an average of 28.1% more for each dollar of FFO from large-cap REITs relative to small-cap REITs. In this monthly publication, I will provide REIT data on numerous metrics to help readers identify which property types and individual securities currently offer the best opportunities to achieve their investment goals.

Micro cap REITs (-8.88%) had a very rough start to the year, whereas their larger peers averaged gains in January. Small caps (+3.27%) led, followed by slightly lower average gains for mid caps (+2.65%) and large caps (+2.35%).

12 out of 18 Property Types Averaged Positive Returns in January

Two-thirds of REIT property types averaged a positive total return in January, with a wide 23.59% total return spread between the best and worst performing property types. Land (+15.36%) and Data Centers (+8.49%) were the best-performing property types in the REIT sector in January. Single Family Housing (-8.24%) and Office (-7.24%) had the poorest start to the year.

The REIT sector as a whole saw the average P/FFO (2026Y) increase from 13.4x to 13.6x during January. 72.2% of property types averaged multiple expansion, 22.2% averaged multiple contraction, and 5.6% saw multiples hold steady. Land (24.8x), Data Centers (23.8x), Manufactured Housing (17.8x), and Timber (17.5x) currently trade at the highest average multiples among REIT property types. Office (7.8x) and Hotels (8.6x) are the only property types that average single-digit FFO multiples.

Performance of Individual Securities

City Office REIT, Inc. (CIO) was taken private and delisted on January 9th after being acquired by MCME Carell at a price of $7.00/share. MCME Carell is a joint venture of Morning Calm Management and Elliot Investment Management.

Plymouth Industrial REIT, Inc. (PLYM) was acquired and taken private by Makarora Management and Ares Management at a price of $22.00/share. PLYM’s final date of trading was January 27th.

Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND) (+22.45%) and Farmland Partners Inc. (FPI) (+20.56%) led the REIT sector in January as investors piled into the only two publicly traded farmland REITs.

Office Properties Income Trust (OPITQ) (-96.09%) continues to see the share price approach zero after a Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing. OPITQ continues to operate as it works to restructure debt and eventually emerge from bankruptcy with a leaner balance sheet and reduced debt service obligations.

63.46% of REITs had a positive total return in January. REITs averaged +1.09% total return in the first month of the year, which is 238 basis points better than the -1.29% return for the REIT sector in January 2025.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Dividend Yield

Dividend yield is an important component of a REIT's total return. The particularly high dividend yields of the REIT sector are, for many investors, the primary reason for investment in this sector. As many REITs are currently trading at share prices well below their NAV, yields are currently quite high for many REITs within the sector. Although a particularly high yield for a REIT may sometimes reflect a disproportionately high risk, there exist opportunities in some cases to capitalize on dividend yields that are sufficiently attractive to justify the underlying risks of the investment. I have included below a table ranking equity REITs from the highest dividend yield (as of 1/31/2026) to the lowest dividend yield.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Although a REIT’s decision regarding whether to pay a quarterly dividend or a monthly dividend does not reflect on the quality of the company’s fundamentals or operations, a monthly dividend allows for smoother cash flow to the investor. Below is a list of equity REITs that pay monthly dividends, ranked from highest yield to lowest yield.

3 REITs raised their dividend in January, one of which is a monthly dividend, and 2 of which are quarterly dividends. The largest increase was from STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG) with a 4% hike, followed by raises of 2.6% and 1% from Modiv Industrial, Inc. (MDV) and Postal Realty Trust, Inc. (PSTL), respectively.

Economic Health

After reaching a 15-year high in 2025, corporate bankruptcies decreased in January both on a month-over-month and year-over-year basis. The number of bankruptcies declined 15.7% from January 2025 and was 21.3% lower than in December. However, even the lower January figure still reflects an environment of elevated bankruptcy filings.

Valuation

REIT Premium/Discount to NAV by Property Type

Below is a downloadable data table, which ranks REITs within each property type from the largest discount to the largest premium to NAV. The consensus NAV used for this table is the average of analyst NAV estimates for each REIT. Both the NAV and the share price will change over time, so I will continue to include this table in upcoming issues of The State of REITs with updated consensus NAV estimates for each REIT for which such an estimate is available.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Takeaway

The large-cap REIT premium (relative to small-cap REITs) widened in January, and investors are now paying on average about 28% more for each dollar of 2026 FFO/share to buy large-cap REITs than small-cap REITs (16.4x/12.8x - 1 = 28.1%). As can be seen in the table below, there is presently a strong positive correlation between market cap and FFO multiple.

The table below shows the average NAV premium/discount of REITs of each market cap bucket. This data, much like the data for price/FFO, shows a strong, positive correlation between market cap and Price/NAV. The average large-cap (-5.81%) and mid-cap REITs (-9.07%) trade at single-digit discounts to consensus NAV. Small-cap REITs (-26.79%) trade around 3/4 of NAV while micro caps (-34.42%) trade for a little less than 2/3 of their respective NAVs.

REITs Engaged in Substantial Transaction Activity in 2025, and the Performance of New REITs Greatly Varied

Numerous REITs went on a buying spree in 2025, with REITs buying a total of 1,956 properties (excluding those acquired through M&A) for a whopping $32.47B. Millrose Properties, Inc. (MRP) bought the most properties (860), followed by Welltower Inc. (WELL) (611) and Bluerock Homes Trust, Inc. (BHM) (274). The REITs that spent the most on property acquisitions were Welltower ($9.806B), W. P. Carey Inc. (WPC) ($1.587B), and Terreno Realty Corporation (TRNO) ($1.106B).

2025 saw a 27.5% decline in the number of property dispositions by REITs. Only 713 properties were sold in 2025. However, the value of properties sold actually increased 66.4% year over year as REITs sold off $24.38 billion of real estate.

The REITs that sold the most properties in 2025 were Sun Communities, Inc. (SUI) (144 properties), W. P. Carey (58 properties), and Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHC) (30 properties). When measured in dollars, however, the REITs with the most property dispositions were Sun Communities ($5.526B), Apartment Investment and Management Company (AIV) ($2.0B), and Elme Communities (ELME) ($1.606B).

There were 3 new publicly traded REITs in 2025, which was only half as many as the year before. It was also only half as many as the number of REITs that no longer trade publicly after being acquired during 2025. The number of publicly traded REITs has steadily declined over the past 5 years, from 182 REITs in January 2021 down to only 155 at the end of January 2026.

There has been a huge disparity in performance of the new 2025 REITs since listing, with an enormous 9780 basis point spread in total return. Millrose Properties, Inc. (MRP) (+41.7%) has significantly outperformed both REITs and the broader market. Fermi Inc. (FRMI) (-56.1%), however, has been a trainwreck and has already collapsed to less than half the IPO price in less than 4 months.

This massive difference in performance highlights the importance of proper due diligence in investing. Simply buying a REIT index means indiscriminately buying both the MRPs of the market and the FRMIs. Building a portfolio of carefully vetted REIT securities allows for capitalizing on unwarranted NAV discounts and targeted investment into REITs with high-quality management teams. Even more importantly, it can help to reduce the risk of investment into REITs with a high likelihood of severe underperformance. In a sector as diverse as REITs, active portfolio management has the potential to achieve substantial alpha.

data.title: BrandywineGLOBAL - Flexible Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The global fixed income market edged higher during the fourth quarter, supported by central bank rate cuts, resilient economic growth, and overall solid investor demand. These factors more than offset sticky inflation, chaotic tariff policies, and a 43-day government shutdown in the U.S. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered its benchmark interest rate 0.25% (25 basis points) at its meetings in September, October, and December. The Bank of England cut rates 25 basis points (bps) in December, while the European Central Bank kept rates steady. The Bank of Japan raised rates 25 bps in December to 1.75%, the highest level in 30 years. The 10-year US Treasury yield rose, ending the quarter three basis points higher at 4.18%. U.S. investment-grade and high-yield credit both posted positive total returns, as did U.S. mortgage-backed securities. Finally, the emerging market debt asset class rallied in the fourth quarter, while emerging market currencies were mixed versus the U.S. dollar.
  • Contributors: Emerging market sovereigns and high-yield credit.
  • Detractors: U.S. duration and exposure to the Japanese yen.
  • Outlook: Corporate credit fundamentals remain constructive, in our view.

Performance Review

  • Emerging market sovereign debt was the top contributor during the quarter, led by Mexico, Argentina, and Egypt. Investor sentiment toward emerging market debt remained constructive, supported by continued demand for carry, while improving external balances in several countries helped underpin performance. Energy-related quasi-sovereigns also added to returns, benefiting from firm commodity prices and disciplined balance sheet management across the sector.
  • U.S. high-yield corporate credit performed well, supported by resilient corporate fundamentals and a supportive technical backdrop. Emerging market hard-currency high-yield corporates also added to performance, as investor demand for spread assets remained steady amid limited new issuance.
  • U.S. prime residential MBS, including credit risk transfer securities, were additive for performance, reflecting stable housing fundamentals, solid credit performance, and continued investor demand for high quality securitized assets.
  • Rates performance was mixed. U.S. Treasury positions detracted from results as yields moved higher over the quarter, driven by firmer inflation data, higher term premiums, and a reassessment of the pace and extent of future Fed easing. UK gilts added modestly to performance as yields declined later in the quarter amid softer growth data and evolving expectations around the policy outlook.
  • The Japanese yen detracted from performance as the currency was pressured by the BoJ’s accommodative policy stance.

Outlook

  • Heading into 2026, we expect developed market bond yields to remain broadly range-bound, extending 2025’s unusually quiet trading conditions, as improving growth impulses are offset by emerging labor market fragility. Reduced tariff drag, supportive fiscal policy in major economies, and favorable financial conditions could underpin activity, but ongoing weakness in employment growth should limit the scope for a sustained move higher in yields and keep central banks attentive to downside risks.
  • Credit fundamentals remain constructive, particularly within high-yield. Corporate balance sheets are generally strong, default rates are running at less than half their historical averages, recoveries have been above average, and overall credit quality has improved. High-yield starting yields in the 6–7% range remain attractive relative to the last decade. While spreads have compressed, the current macro backdrop—characterized by range-bound economic growth, supportive tax policy, and an accommodative Fed—continue to favor the asset class. However, with spreads largely range-bound since mid-2023 and new issuance increasing, we favor a selective approach. Absent a sustained bear market in equities or core fixed income, we expect this spread range to hold. Short-dated high-yield bonds remain appealing given their elevated income, limited duration risk, and pull-to-par characteristics.
  • Emerging markets also continue to offer selective opportunities. Latin America sovereign markets benefit from elevated real yields and improving fiscal dynamics in select markets. A softer U.S. dollar over the medium term and the potential for gradual easing in global monetary policy should provide additional support for emerging market assets.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of 1xed income securities falls. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Leverage increases the volatility of investment returns and subjects investments to magnified losses and a decline in value. Active and frequent trading may increase a shareholder’s tax liability and transaction costs. The portfolio is non-diversified and may invest in a relatively small number of issuers, which may negatively impact the fund’s performance and result in greater fluctuation in the value of the fund’s shares. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

A Credit Risk Transfer (CRT) is a channel for government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to transfer credit risk to private investors and away from taxpayers.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of a bond’s price to changes in interest rates.

Gilts are bonds that are issued by the British government, and they are generally considered low-risk investments.
